entry.title: SAP BTP上でLangChainアプリケーションを開発・実行・監視する
entry.content:
はじめに

生成AIの活用が急速に進む現代において、ビジネスプロセスの効率化や付加価値の創出を目指して、AI技術を統合したアプリケーションの需要が高まっています。SAP S/4HANA Cloudを中心とする業務システムの拡張開発基盤として、SAP BTP (Business Technology Platform) はその柔軟性とスケーラビリティから多くの注目を集めています。特に、SAP BTP上で生成AI（Generative AI）を活用したアプリケーションを構築することは、新たな可能性を切り開く鍵となるでしょう。

本ブログでは、SAP BTP上でLLM（Large Language Model）アプリケーションを構築・運用・監視する手順を、ステップバイステップで詳しく解説します。Azure OpenAIが提供する「gpt-4o」モデルを例に、SAP AI Launchpadを利用したLLMアクセス設定から、SAP Build Codeを用いたLangChainアプリケーションのデプロイ、さらにLangfuseを活用した監視方法までを網羅します。これにより、SAP BTPを最大限に活用した拡張可能なAIソリューションの構築方法を理解することができます。

このブログを参考にしていただくことで、生成AIを取り入れたアプリケーション開発における技術的な基礎を学び、実際のビジネスシナリオに応用するための一歩を踏み出していただけることを願っています。

手順

前提条件

  • SAP AI Launchpad / SAP AI Core (Extended Plan) / SAP Build Codeの有効化が済んでいる
  • SAP Build Codeを用いてFull-Stack ApplicationタイプのDevSpaceが作成されている

1. Generative AI Hubを用いて強力なLLMへアクセスする

SAP AI LaunchpadからGenerative AI Hubにアクセスし、外部の強力なLLMに対するアクセスを設定していきます。まずはどのようなモデルが利用可能かについては、下記のSAP Noteにまとめて記載があります。必要に応じて確認してください。

https://me.sap.com/notes/3437766

今回は Azure OpenAI の元で提供されている 「gpt-4o」 というモデルを利用していきます。

まずSAP AI Launchpadで作業用のリソースグループに入り、左側のペイン内から 「ML運用」=>「設定」 へ移動します。右上の「作成」より、LLMアクセス設定の作成を行います。

次に、画像の通りに入力を行います。名前は任意ですが、今回は「gpt-4o」としています。

次のページでは、利用するLLMの種類とバージョンを設定します。このmodelNameとmodelVersionには、上記SAP Noteで確認できるモデルとバージョンの名前を入力してください。

以上で設定は完了です。そのまま順次進むと、下記の画面が現れます。modelNameが「gpt-4o」となっていることを確認してください。問題なければ、この起動設定に基づいて、右上のボタンより「デプロイメント作成」 を行います。

起動時間などの設定が可能ですが、今回は全てデフォルトの設定で進めます。うまくいくと、下記の画面に遷移するはずです。しばらく待つとこのLLMアクセスポイントが作成・起動し、現在のステータスが「実行中」 となります。

この状態になれば、Generative AI Hubの設定は完了となります。

なお、下記で実装するアプリケーションを実行する場合には、「gpt-4o」だけでなく、「text-embedding-ada-002」モデルを同様の手順でデプロイしてください。

 

2. SAP Build CodeでLangChainのアプリケーションを用意し、SAP BTP上にあらかじめ起動したLangfuseへ接続を行う

続いて、SAP Build Codeを用いてLangChainのアプリケーションをSAP BTP, Cloud Foundry Runtimeにデプロイしていきます。前回のブログで、LangfuseというLLMアプリケーションの監視用プラットフォームをSAP BTP, Kyma Runtime上にデプロイしました。もしまだご覧になっていない方は、合わせてご参照ください。

LLM監視ツールLangfuseをSAP BTP, Kyma runtimeにデプロイしてみた 

今回は、GitHubからサンプルアプリケーションをクローンして利用しましょう。

git clone https://github.com/watwatwhat/Langfuse_LangChain_onSAPBTP.git

 

 

今回のアプリケーションはMTA（Multi Target Application）として構成されており、SAP Build Code上でデフォルトで利用可能なコマンドラインツールを用いて簡単にデプロイすることができます。

ここで、ソースコードの重要なポイントをピックアップして強調しておきます。

Generative AI Hub SDKを用いてSAP AI Core経由でLangChainを利用する部分

 

from gen_ai_hub.proxy.langchain.openai import OpenAIEmbeddings as g_OpenAIEmbeddings
from gen_ai_hub.proxy.langchain.openai import ChatOpenAI as g_ChatOpenAI

 

 

proxy_client = get_proxy_client('gen-ai-hub')  # <- GenAIHubプロキシ
embeddings = g_OpenAIEmbeddings(proxy_model_name=embedding_model)  # <- SAP AI Core経由のOpenAI Embeddingモデル
llm = g_ChatOpenAI(proxy_model_name=model_option, proxy_client=proxy_client, temperature=0.0)  # <- SAP AI Core経由のOpenAI Chatモデル

 

Python向けに提供されているGenerative AI Hub SDKを介してSAP AI Coreを経由してLLMにアクセスすることができます。

Langfuseへのログ流し込みを行う部分

 

agent = create_react_agent(
    llm=llm,
    tools=tools,
    prompt=prompt
)
agent_executor = AgentExecutor(
    agent=agent,
    tools=tools, 
    handle_parsing_errors=True, 
    verbose=True,
    max_iterations=5
)
from langfuse.callback import CallbackHandler
langfuse_handler = CallbackHandler(
    public_key="XXXXXXX",
    secret_key="XXXXXXX",
    host="http://XXXXXXXX:3000"
)

response = agent_executor.invoke({"input": input}, config={"callbacks": [langfuse_handler]})

 

langfuseパッケージにより提供されるコールバックハンドラをAgentExecutorのコールバックとして渡すことで、Chainの中でこのハンドラを経由してログがLangfuseへと送信されるようになります。

なお、この接続情報は、LangfuseのUIのダッシュボードページから、右上の 「Configure Tracing」というボタンをクリックすると遷移する下記のページから取得することができます。今回はLangChain上で利用をするため、LangChainのタブを開いてコードスニペットをコピー&ペーストすることで設定ができます。

それではデプロイをしていきましょう。クローンしたディレクトリに入り、下記のコマンドを実行してビルドを行います。

 

mbt build

 

続いて、下記コマンドを用いて、生成されたmtarファイルをSAP BTP, Cloud Foundry Runtimeにデプロイします。cf login後にはデプロイ先のOrganizationやSpaceを指定してください。

 

cf login
cf deploy mta_archives/langfuse-test-app_1.0.0.mtar

 

デプロイが完了すると、インターネットからこのアプリケーションにアクセスすることができます。下記のコマンドを用いて、このアプリケーションのURLを取得しましょう。

 

cf apps

 

今回は 「langfuse-langchain-srv」 という名前で公開しており、横にURLが確認できます。

それでは、このLangChainアプリケーションを実行してみましょう。

3. LangChainによる推論チェーンを実行し、Langfuseからトレースを確認する

今回はローカルのPCにインストールしたPostmanからこのPythonアプリケーションにリクエストを行います。実行を行うと、SAP BTP, Cloud Foundry Runtime上でAIエージェントが推論を行い、結果が返されます。

今回はLangfuseによるトレースハンドラを仕込んでいますので、LangfuseのUIからトレースの確認を行いましょう。Langfuse UIから 「Tracing」 => 「Traces」 へ移動します。すると、下記のようにトレースの一覧が確認できます。

中身を確認してみましょう。まず、右側にあるペインがLangChainで実装したAIエージェントがどのように行動したのか、というシーケンスです。1番最上位の要素を選択すると、左側に表示されるような、inputとoutputの確認ができます。

右側のペインをなぞっていくと、「計算機」 を利用していることが確認できます。これはLangChainのソースコードの中でツールとしてAIエージェントに渡したものであり、今回タスクとしてAIエージェントに渡した 「3の3乗根は？」 という問題では、計算機での計算を行うことが望ましいアクションであるため、AIエージェントが選択して利用したのだと考えられます。これを選択すると、下記のようにこのツールに対してどのようにinputされ、ツールの実行結果としてどのようなoutputが得られたのか？を確認することもできます。

もちろん、ダッシュボードの方にも統計情報が反映されています。

 

今回は計算機というツールだけを用いたシナリオであったため、質問・回答・ログ、共にシンプルなものとなりましたが、実際のユースケースではさらに複雑な形になるでしょう。そういったシナリオでは、本ブログでご紹介している製品群がさらに大きな効果を発揮するはずです。

おわりに

本ブログでは、SAP BTPを活用してLLMアプリケーションを開発・実行・監視する手順を紹介しました。SAP AI Launchpadを利用したGenerative AI Hubの設定から、SAP Build Codeを用いたLangChainアプリケーションのデプロイ、Langfuseを用いたトレーシングや監視まで、一連の流れを具体的に解説することで、SAP BTPの持つポテンシャルを感じていただけたのではないでしょうか。

生成AIを統合したアプリケーションは、従来の業務プロセスに新たな価値をもたらし、より効率的で柔軟なシステム運用を可能にします。また、Langfuseのような監視ツールを活用することで、AIの動作や推論結果を詳細に分析し、信頼性の向上や運用の最適化が実現できます。

これからも、生成AIやLLMの技術は進化し続け、さまざまなユースケースでの活用が期待されます。SAP BTPを基盤としたアプリケーション開発を通じて、企業のDX（デジタルトランスフォーメーション）をさらに加速させる取り組みをぜひ進めてみてください。本ブログがその第一歩となることを願っています。

entry.title: Overcoming the limitations of standard RAG approaches with advanced techniques
entry.content:
Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) has become an essential tactic for improving the performance of Large Language Models (LLMs) and represents a significant milestone in the AI journey we started about a year ago, and we have successfully implemented it in various customer use cases. However, the standard RAG approaches have their limitations, which the advanced RAG techniques aim to address. This blog post addresses these limitations and shows how advanced methods can provide robust solutions.Standard RAG Approach

Challenges with Standard RAG Approaches

  •  Simplicity and Limited Customization

The basic RAG approach, as shown in the first image, primarily involves a retrieval step, a prompt and an LLM. The system relies heavily on searching for top-k documents based on similarity scores. While this approach is simple, it offers limited customization options as only the value of k can be adjusted. This simplicity can lead to sub-optimal results, especially for complex queries that require a more differentiated search.

  •  The Lost-in-the-Middle Problem

A common issue with traditional RAG models is the ‘lost-in-the-middle’ problem, which is highlighted in a study by An et al. (2024) entitled ‘Make Your LLM Fully Utilize the Context’. Although LLMs process large inputs, they are often unable to utilize the full context effectively due to insufficient monitoring during training. This is reflected in performance degradation in document searches, code function searches and database entity searches where critical information is ignored, especially in the middle segments of the input data.

  •  Ineffective Information Utilization

As mentioned in the work of Guo et al. (2024), the traditional RAG process does not compress or organize information efficiently, which often results in less relevant data being processed, which can affect the performance of LLMs. This inefficiency emphasizes the need for a more sophisticated mechanism that can rank information based on its relevance and importance.

 

Advanced RAG Approaches: A Solution

  • Implementation of the LOTR Merger Retriever

A key solution to the lost-in-the-middle problem is the use of Lord of the Retriever (LOTR - pun intended), also known as MergerRetriever. LOTR improves the retrieval process by merging the results of multiple retrievers, ensuring that all sections of the input are considered and not just the beginning or end. This method prevents critical data, often found in the middle of the text, from being overlooked.

Similar to how we might remember the first and last items on a shopping list but forget the middle ones, traditional RAG systems fail to capture important details in the middle of a document. LOTR solves this problem by combining the results of different retrievers and prioritizing the most relevant documents. This leads to more balanced and accurate answers and ensures that no part of the data is overlooked, whether it is the beginning, middle or end.

By integrating LOTR into the RAG process, we improve the system's ability to process complex data sets and ensure a more comprehensive understanding of the information retrieved.

  • Information Compression with Ranking

The advanced RAG approach, which is inspired by innovative methods such as those of Guo et al. (2024), uses a ranking mechanism based on lossless data compression. In this way, the compression ratio and thus the performance of LLMs is significantly improved. A broader range of documents (top k: 20) is selected and narrowed down to the most relevant group (top k: 5) to ensure that only essential information is processed.

Information Compression with Ranking

 

  • Incorporation of an Evaluator

An additional layer of the advanced RAG model is the evaluator, which assigns points to documents based on their relevance and hallucination potential. This scoring system ensures that the responses generated closely match the relevant context and reduces possible inaccuracies, making the results of the LLM more reliable.

  •  Enhanced Customization and Control

In contrast to the basic approach, the advanced RAG model offers more parameters for customization - top_k_retrieval and top_K_ranker - and introduces rigorous evaluation metrics such as document relevance, hallucination and similarity scores. This flexibility enables a more tailored retrieval process that maximizes the utility and accuracy of LLM output.

Evaluation metrics

The evaluation of RAG outputs is crucial to understanding their effectiveness. As outlined in the RAG evaluation methods, metrics include:

- Response vs. reference response: Measures correctness and similarity to ground truth.

- Response vs. input context: Assesses the relevance and usefulness of the response.

- Response vs. Retrieved Documents: Assesses reliability and possible hallucinations.

These scoring protocols ensure that advanced RAG solutions not only retrieve the responses, but also put them in the right context to the user queries.

Advanced RAG Approach

Conclusion

Advanced RAG approaches represent a significant leap forward by addressing the inherent limitations of standard RAG models. By introducing compression-based ranking and improved evaluation metrics, these solutions promise a more efficient and accurate information retrieval process that increases the overall utility of LLMs in various applications.

As research continues, these advances will further refine the approach to large language models and ensure increasingly better results for complex data retrieval tasks.

entry.title: Extracting data from screenshots (with and without AI)
entry.content:
We've probably all been there dozens of times:
Having humbly requested information from a co-worker in the unshakable faith one possesses in their colleague's thoughtfulness, envisioning the forthcoming data neatly packaged in copy-paste-ready text. Only to see that faith crumble into a thousand despairing fragments upon the realization that they have gallantly sent... a screenshot?! 😕

While one surely could ask the aforementioned colleague to export the data properly on his end and resend it, there is no telling when he/she might come back. It might even be the next day. Trying to evangelize people into ideally not sending screenshots in such cases is only worth trying if you collaborate with them on a regular basis.

Transcribing the screenshot yourself is (assuming any reasonable amount of data - let's say 10+ material numbers) not an option as its dull & time consuming work and - let's be honest - feels like admitting defeat for any remotely tech-savvy human being.

So, what other options are there?
I tried the ones that seemed most obvious and accessible to me - so you don't have to.  

 

Screen grabber (TechSmith Snagit):

Allows you to extract text from a subselection or from whole screenshot (which even preserves table structures) or a sub-area.
While this method is rather quick, the accuracy is not convincing, as it has a tendency to confuse "O", "0" and  "Q".

 

MS OneNote: Copy Text from Picture 

Also OneNote offers a rudimentary extract text functionality. Here you can only extract all text from a whole image. OneNote's "Copy Text from Picture" displays the same tendency to confuse "O", "0" and  "Q". Table structures are not preserved. Instead it seems to interpret table borders crossing as the letter "L". It also occasionally omits portions of material numbers:

 

Mobile phone standard apps (Android Gallery) 

Turns out, even the standard "Gallery" app on Android 13+ is capable of extracting text. After taping the "Extract Text" button ("T" - bottom right), it will highlight all text it recognizes, whereafter you can select the text and copy it to the clipboard. Accurately selecting multiple bits of text is quite tedious though. Also it confused "Q" an "O" in one case.

Being an android person, I could not try this so far but the standard "Photos" app on iOS 15+ should have a similar capability called "Live Text" (has to be enabled in setting). 

 

 Large Language Models (LLMs)

Most of the younger large language models are also capable of processing images as input data - so why not give them a spin as well? At least with the examples I used, I found describing in natural language what to extract once and not having to select/copy/paste n times to be generally more convenient. While all LLMs I tried were able to "understand" the task and correctly identified the information to extract, there were some differences in accuracy. I used SAP AI Launchpad to access all of the LLMs except for CoPilot.

Anthropic Claude-3-Opus

...displayed a similar tendency to confuse "Q", "O" and "0" as observed with other methods:

 

Anthropic Claude-3-Haiku

While accurately recognizing "Q", "O" and "0", Haiku tends to (though rarely) sneak in additional digits:

 

 

Anthropic Claude-3.5-sonnet / OpenAI GPT 4o / Google Gemini-1.5-flash / MS Copilot

All these models accurately recognized & returned the text from my sample screenshots:

As this was my first time trying MS Copilot I was naively expecting it to be more deeply integrated in Windows and capable of accessing files and mails but you have to copy/paste the screenshot in the prompt (or upload it) just like with any other LLM: 

 

TL;DR

First of all, keep in mind the sample size of this little experiment is rather small (<5 screenshots total) so you likely will stumble on different issues.
Still - based on that small sample: Assuming all alternatives are accessible, the younger LLMs seem to offer the best mix of ease of use and accuracy. However, even those are not perfect: For example all LLMs missed suffixes in material numbers containing several spaces.
While their use definitely saves time, the results still should be double checked. 

PS: If you use other methods, feel free to mention them in a comment.

entry.title: SAP Developer News, December 19th, 2024
entry.content:
Podcast: https://podcast.opensap.info/sap-developers/2024/12/19/sap-developer-news-december-19th-2024/

DESCRIPTION

SAP Developer News End of Year Schedule

New in SAP SDKs for AI

  • SAP Cloud SDK for AI (JavaScript): https://github.com/SAP/ai-sdk-js/releases/
  • SAP Cloud SDK for AI (Java): https://github.com/SAP/ai-sdk-java/releases
  • Generative AI Hub SDK (Python): https://help.sap.com/doc/generative-ai-hub-sdk/CLOUD
  • Understand the World of AI with SAP Learning: https://community.sap.com/t5/artificial-intelligence-and-machine-learning-blogs/understand-the-world-of-ai-with-sap-learning/ba-p/13968267

New series: The Art and Science of CAP with Daniel Hutzel

  • Episodes and show notes https://qmacro.org/blog/posts/2024/12/06/the-art-and-science-of-cap/
  • Live stream series playlist https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PL6RpkC85SLQAe45xlhIfhTYB9G0mdRVjI

Extending SAPUI5 Apps

  • Blog post: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blogs-by-sap/extending-sapui5-apps-adaptation-projects-vs-extension-projects/ba-p/13961264

SAP Data & Analytics Advisory Methodology

  • SAP Data & Analytics Advisory Methodology Overview | SAP Help Portal: https://help.sap.com/docs/sap-btp-guidance-framework/sap-data-methodology/sap-data-analytics-advisory-methodology-overview
  • Highlights of SAP Datasphere in 2024: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/activity-7275068742051450881-iHTy
  • Watch out for our Q4 release for SAP HANA Cloud: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/thomashammersap_saphanacloud-sapbtp-saphana-activity-7272528046564282368-t2GE

CHAPTER TITLES

0:00 Intro
0:10 Dev News Schedule
1:01 New in SAP SDKs for AI
2:27 New series: The Art and Science of CAP with Daniel Hutzel
3:52 Extending SAPUI5 Apps
4:49 SAP Data & Analytics Advisory Methodology

Transcription

[Intro] This is the SAP Developer News for December 19th, 2024.

[Tom] I just want to let everybody know about the upcoming plans for the developer news as we take our annual end of year break. This is the last regular news episode that we'll have for 2024. And like the previous years, we have special year and review episodes coming up for the last week of December and the first week of December. January. So you hear from each of the team members about what they reflect upon 2024, their major accomplishments, things that they've enjoyed, all those sort of things. And be on the lookout for our special bloopers. We'll throw some fun, exciting bloopers in at the end of each of those episodes and probably release that separately as well. So we look forward to seeing you back here for new episodes the second week of January 2025.

[Witalij] SAP Cloud SDK for AI is our official software development kit for SAP AI core, SAP Generative AI hub and orchestration service. The JavaScript TypeScript version was introduced two months ago, and last week we released version 1.4. This new version odds support for the orchestration services grounding capabilities. Grounding allows you to integrate external, contextually, relevant, domain-specific or real-time data into your AI processes. This data supplements the natural language processing capabilities of pre-trained models, which are typically trained only on general data sets. Additionally, the Java version of the SDK has reached a significant milestone with the release of version 1.0, bringing numerous new features and enhancements. You can find the complete documentation and sample projects in the Git repository. For Python users, the Generative AI Hub SDK has recently released version 4.1.1, which is a new introduces support for prompt registry templates in orchestration. I hear feeling overwhelmed by all these terminology. No worries. If you are new to AI are wondering how SAP is embedding AI into our portfolio, check out the  recent post by my teammate Nora. She guides you through relevant courses and activities available on SAP learning. Links are provided in the description.

[DJ] If you've ever wanted to gain a deeper of CAP. Learn more about its design and the fundamental core computing building blocks upon which it's based. Then we've got a series for you. On the Hands on SAP Dev show, which we've been running since early 2019, we've got a new mini -series with none other than the creator of CAP himself, Daniel Hutzel. The series is called the Art and Science of CAP. and in each episode, Daniel takes us on journeys deep and wide, bringing enlightenment and wonder in equal measure. Now, the series was kicked off during Devtoberfest 2024. It's still running. There's an episode or two to go. And alongside the episodes, the live stream episodes, there are also show notes that you can dive into afterwards. Or you can just watch the replays of the live stream episodes. and dive into the show notes as well. There's a ton of stuff to explore and learn about. So, this holiday season, treat yourself to some amazing insights and check out the episodes that we have on offer along with your company notes. And we'll see you online.

[Michelle] Hey y'all. If you're a developer working with SAPUI5, you want to check out this blog post about extension projects versus adaption projects. It's all about how SAP is moving towards a more advanced way of customizing apps with adapt projects. The post explains how Adaption Projects created an SAP Business Application Studio lets you adapt apps without modifying the standard code. You get powerful new features, improved upgrade stability, and the flexibility to work with on any SAP app, not just legacy ones. It also compares the older extension projects to adaption projects showing why adaption projects are the way forward and to give you a better idea of the differences between the two. If you're working with existing extension projects, don't worry, you can still use them, but SAP recommends migrating to an adaption project for bigger or new requirements. To learn more about the blog post, it's linked below and as always, happy coding.

[Witalij] Just in time for the year end, we are happy to share that the SAP Data and Analytics advisory methodology is now officially available as part of the SAP Help Documentation. The methodology provides a comprehensive framework for enterprise and data architects to evaluate data strategies, develop solution architectures and foster a data-driven culture through a structured four-phase approach. This process is supported by a data domain reference model to establish a common understanding of SAP data, a data and analytics capability model to enhance the architectural definition process, and data-driven use-case patterns and reference architecture. to accelerate solution architecture development. Use the holiday season as an opportunity to get inspired and set the groundwork for successful data and analytics projects in the coming year. And while beyond the topic, I'd like to highlight two product-specific resources from the respective managers. The SAP DataSphere 2024 highlights presented by KP Sauer and the teaser for the Q4 release of SAP HANA cloud shared by Thomas Hammer. The links are in the description, as always.

entry.title: S4 HANA Graph Workspace with LangChain
entry.content:
Introduction

In today's world, data is extremely valuable, and being able to understand and use large amounts of information gives organizations a big advantage. Graphs are one of the best tools to help with this. They arrange data in a way that makes it easier to understand, explore, and analyze. This blog explores how to use graphs with Hana DB and GenAI features.

Prerequisites

  • SAP AI Core access and anthropic--claude-3.5-sonnet model deployed.
  • SAP HANA DB access.

The Core Components of a Graph

NODE: The node in a graph, represents entities like people, places, organizations, or concepts.

RELATIONSHIPS: The connections between entities that define how they are related or interact with each other.

ATTRIBUTES: Additional information about entities or relationships that provide further context.

SCHEMA: A formal representation of the knowledge within a specific domain, defining the types of entities, relationships, and the rules governing their interrelations.

Through this blog we will see how we can use HANA DB and Large Language Models to implement Graph which can provide with the answer to questions asked using natural language.

  • Create HANA DB graph workspace.
  • Insert data and data in vector format.
  • Generate the cypher query which is supported by SAP HANA Graph Workspace
  • Execute the query and get the answer.

Part 1 : Create Graph Workspace and Upload Data

For detailed explanation please refer this tutorial: Create an SAP HANA Graph Workspace | SAP Tutorials

 

Step 1 : Create two tables for Nodes and Edges of the Graph.

 

CREATE TABLE "ERP_NODES"(
	"NODE_ID"  NVARCHAR(5000) NOT NULL,
	"DESCRIPTION" NVARCHAR(5000),
	"NAME" NVARCHAR(5000),
	"NODE_TYPE" NVARCHAR(5000),
	"NODE_VECTOR" REAL_VECTOR,
	PRIMARY KEY (
		"NODE_ID"
	)
);

CREATE TABLE "ERP_EDGES"(
	"EDGE_ID"  NVARCHAR(5000) NOT NULL,
	"SOURCE" NVARCHAR(5000) NOT NULL,
	"DESTINATION"NVARCHAR(5000) NOT NULL,
	"RELATIONSHIP_TYPE" NVARCHAR(5000),
	"EDGE_VECTOR" REAL_VECTOR,
	PRIMARY KEY (
		"EDGE_ID"
	)
);

 

 

Step 2 :  Create Graph Work space

 

ALTER TABLE "ERP_EDGES" ADD FOREIGN KEY ("SOURCE") REFERENCES "ERP_NODES" ("NODE_ID") ON UPDATE CASCADE ON DELETE CASCADE ENFORCED VALIDATED;

ALTER TABLE "ERP_EDGES" ADD FOREIGN KEY ( "DESTINATION" ) REFERENCES "ERP_NODES" ("NODE_ID") ON UPDATE CASCADE ON DELETE CASCADE ENFORCED VALIDATED;

CREATE GRAPH WORKSPACE "ERP"
  EDGE TABLE "ERP_EDGES"
    SOURCE COLUMN "SOURCE"
    TARGET COLUMN "DESTINATION"
    KEY COLUMN "EDGE_ID"
  VERTEX TABLE "ERP_NODES"
    KEY COLUMN "NODE_ID";

 

 

Step 3: Download the data from here

Let's consider the following graph example for our case.

 

This excel contains sheets per node and edge. Node (Entities) sheet depicts Node with NODE_ID and Description.  Edge (relationships) sheet depicts how two nodes are related to each other.

 

Step 4: Upload the data using following code.

CODE:

 

import pandas as pd
from hdbcli import dbapi
import uuid
from gen_ai_hub.proxy.langchain.init_models import init_embedding_model
from gen_ai_hub.proxy import GenAIHubProxyClient
import os
from langchain_community.callbacks import get_openai_callback
os.environ["AICORE_CLIENT_ID"] = "AICORE_CLIENT_ID"
os.environ["AICORE_CLIENT_SECRET"] = "AICORE_CLIENT_SECRET"
os.environ["AICORE_AUTH_URL"] = "AICORE_AUTH_URL"
os.environ["AICORE_BASE_URL"] = "AICORE_BASE_URL"
os.environ["AICORE_RESOURCE_GROUP"] = "AICORE_RESOURCE_GROUP"
gen_ai_hub_proxy_client = GenAIHubProxyClient(
    base_url="AICORE_BASE_URL",
    auth_url="AICORE_AUTH_URL",
    client_id="AICORE_CLIENT_ID",
    client_secret="AICORE_CLIENT_SECRET",
    resource_group="AICORE_RESOURCE_GROUP"
)
embeddings = init_embedding_model('text-embedding-ada-002')
HANA_DB_ADDRESS = "HANA_DB_ADDRESS"
HANA_DB_PORT = "HANA_DB_PORT"
HANA_DB_USER = "HANA_DB_USER"
HANA_DB_PASSWORD ="HANA_DB_PASSWORD"
 
driver = dbapi.connect(
    address=HANA_DB_ADDRESS, 
    port=HANA_DB_PORT, 
    user=HANA_DB_USER, 
    password=HANA_DB_PASSWORD
)
cursor = driver.cursor()
# Read data from Excel
excel_file = "data_blog.xlsx"  # Replace with your Excel file path
 
customer_df = pd.read_excel(excel_file, sheet_name='Customer')
order_df = pd.read_excel(excel_file, sheet_name='Order')
product_df = pd.read_excel(excel_file, sheet_name='Product')
supplier_df = pd.read_excel(excel_file, sheet_name='Supplier')
category_df = pd.read_excel(excel_file, sheet_name='Category')
customer_order_df = pd.read_excel(excel_file, sheet_name='Customer_Order')
order_product_df = pd.read_excel(excel_file, sheet_name='Order_Product')
product_supplier_df = pd.read_excel(excel_file, sheet_name='Product_Supplier')
product_category_df = pd.read_excel(excel_file, sheet_name='Product_Category')

 
# Function to create nodes
def create_node(cursor, id, description,node_type, node_vector):
    query = 'INSERT INTO ERP_NODES (NODE_ID, DESCRIPTION, NAME, NODE_TYPE, NODE_VECTOR) VALUES (?, ?, ?, ?, TO_REAL_VECTOR(?))'
    cursor.execute(query, (id, description, description, node_type, node_vector))
 
# Function to create relationships
def create_relationship(cursor, id, source, destination, relationship_type, edge_vector):
    query = 'INSERT INTO ERP_EDGES (EDGE_ID, SOURCE, DESTINATION, RELATIONSHIP_TYPE, EDGE_VECTOR) VALUES (?, ?, ?, ?, TO_REAL_VECTOR(?))'
    cursor.execute(query, (id, source, destination, relationship_type,edge_vector))
 
# Create Customer nodes
for index, row in customer_df.iterrows():
    if pd.notna(row['ID']) and pd.notna(row['Description']):
        node_vector = str(embeddings.embed_query(row['Description']))
        create_node(cursor=cursor, id=row['ID'], description=row['Description'], node_type="Customer",node_vector=node_vector)

# Create Order nodes
for index, row in order_df.iterrows():
    if pd.notna(row['ID']) and pd.notna(row['Description']):
        node_vector = str(embeddings.embed_query(row['Description']))
        create_node(cursor=cursor, id=row['ID'], description=row['Description'], node_type="Order",node_vector=node_vector)
        

# Create Product nodes
for index, row in product_df.iterrows():
    if pd.notna(row['ID']) and pd.notna(row['Description']):
        node_vector = str(embeddings.embed_query(row['Description']))
        create_node(cursor=cursor, id=row['ID'], description=row['Description'], node_type="Product",node_vector=node_vector)
        

# Create Supplier nodes
for index, row in supplier_df.iterrows():
    if pd.notna(row['ID']) and pd.notna(row['Description']):
        node_vector = str(embeddings.embed_query(row['Description']))
        create_node(cursor=cursor, id=row['ID'], description=row['Description'], node_type="Supplier",node_vector=node_vector)
        
# Create Category nodes
for index, row in category_df.iterrows():
    if pd.notna(row['ID']) and pd.notna(row['Description']):
        node_vector = str(embeddings.embed_query(row['Description']))
        create_node(cursor=cursor, id=row['ID'], description=row['Description'], node_type="Category",node_vector=node_vector)

# Create relationships between Customer and Order
for index, row in customer_order_df.iterrows():
    if pd.notna(row['Customer_ID']) and pd.notna(row['Order_ID']):
        customer_places_order_edge_vector = str(embeddings.embed_query("CUSTOMER_PLACES_ORDER"))
        order_associated_to_customer_edge_vector = str(embeddings.embed_query("ORDER_ASSOCIATED_TO_CUSTOMER"))
        create_relationship(cursor=cursor, id=str(uuid.uuid4()), source=row['Customer_ID'], destination=row['Order_ID'], relationship_type="CUSTOMER_PLACES_ORDER", edge_vector=customer_places_order_edge_vector)
        create_relationship(cursor=cursor, id=str(uuid.uuid4()), source=row['Order_ID'], destination=row['Customer_ID'], relationship_type="ORDER_ASSOCIATED_TO_CUSTOMER", edge_vector=order_associated_to_customer_edge_vector)
        

# Create relationships between Order and Product
for index, row in order_product_df.iterrows():
    if pd.notna(row['Order_ID']) and pd.notna(row['Product_ID']):
        order_has_product_edge_vector = str(embeddings.embed_query("ORDER_HAS_PRODUCTS"))
        create_relationship(cursor=cursor, id=str(uuid.uuid4()), source=row['Order_ID'], destination=row['Product_ID'], relationship_type="ORDER_HAS_PRODUCTS", edge_vector=order_has_product_edge_vector)
        

# Create relationships between Product and Supplier
for index, row in product_supplier_df.iterrows():
    if pd.notna(row['Product_ID']) and pd.notna(row['Supplier_ID']):
        product_supplied_by_edge_vector = str(embeddings.embed_query("PRODUCT_SUPPLIED_BY"))
        supplier_supplies_product_edge_vector = str(embeddings.embed_query("SUPPLIER_SUPPLIES_PRODUCT"))
        create_relationship(cursor=cursor, id=str(uuid.uuid4()), source=row['Product_ID'], destination=row['Supplier_ID'], relationship_type="PRODUCT_SUPPLIED_BY", edge_vector=product_supplied_by_edge_vector)
        create_relationship(cursor=cursor, id=str(uuid.uuid4()), source=row['Supplier_ID'], destination=row['Product_ID'], relationship_type="SUPPLIER_SUPPLIES_PRODUCT", edge_vector=supplier_supplies_product_edge_vector)
      
# Create relationships between Product and Category
for index, row in product_category_df.iterrows():
    if pd.notna(row['Product_ID']) and pd.notna(row['Category_ID']):
        product_belongs_to_category_edge_vector = str(embeddings.embed_query("PRODUCT_BELONGS_TO_CATEGORY"))
        category_has_products_edge_vector = str(embeddings.embed_query("CATEGORY_HAS_PRODUCTS"))
        create_relationship(cursor=cursor, id=str(uuid.uuid4()), source=row['Product_ID'], destination=row['Category_ID'], relationship_type="PRODUCT_BELONGS_TO_CATEGORY", edge_vector=product_belongs_to_category_edge_vector)
        create_relationship(cursor=cursor, id=str(uuid.uuid4()), source=row['Category_ID'], destination=row['Product_ID'], relationship_type="CATEGORY_HAS_PRODUCTS", edge_vector=category_has_products_edge_vector)

cursor.close()

 

 

Part 2 : Generate cypher query from English language and fetching data from database

Let’s take a look at the problems we will have to address to generate cypher query from English language.

  1. Generate cypher query using Large Language Model
  2. How to identify unspecified data from query

Generate cypher query using Large Language Model

Prompt is helpful in generating cypher query from English language. We will have to provide the rules and schema to the LLM to generate schema.

SCHEMA:

 

[
	{ Node:  
		[  
		{ name:NODE_TYPE, data_type: STRING }
		{ name:DESCRIPTION, data_type: STRING } ,   
		{ name:NODE_ID, data_type: STRING }    
		predefined list of NODE_TYPE:[ 
							Customer, 
							Order,
							Product, 
							Category,   
							Supplier
							]
		]   
	}
]

Relationships: 
[ 
	{ CUSTOMER_PLACES_ORDER: [ source: Node(NODE_TYPE: Customer), destination: Node(NODE_TYPE: Order) ], description : ' Customer places one or many orders '}, 
	{ ORDER_ASSOCIATED_TO_CUSTOMER: [ source: Node(NODE_TYPE: Order), destination: Node(NODE_TYPE: Customer) ], description : ' Order is associated with Customer'}, 
	{ ORDER_HAS_PRODUCTS : [ source: Node(NODE_TYPE: Order), destination: Node(NODE_TYPE: Product) ], description : ' Order contains one or more products '}, 
	{ PRODUCT_SUPPLIED_BY: [ source: Node(NODE_TYPE: Product), destination: Node(NODE_TYPE: Supplier) ], description : ' Product is supplied by one or more suppliers '}, 
	{ SUPPLIER_SUPPLIES_PRODUCT: [ source: Node(NODE_TYPE: Supplier), destination: Node(NODE_TYPE: Product) ], description : ' Product is supplied by one or more suppliers }, 
	{ PRODUCT_BELONGS_TO_CATEGORY: [ source: Node(NODE_TYPE: Product), destination: Node(NODE_TYPE: Category) ], description : ' Product belongs to a specific category '},
	{ CATEGORY_HAS_PRODUCTS: [ source: Node(NODE_TYPE: Category), destination: Node(NODE_TYPE: Product) ], description : ' Category has one or more product '},
]

 

PROMPT:

 

You need to check all the relationships before generating query. Relationships and query may not have exact wordings. 
RULES: 
1. For the generation of open cypher query use node fields NODE_ID and DESCRIPTION in condition of NODE and generate cypher query only using provided relationships and schema.
2. There can be chances where, to reach final output one need to traverse multiple nodes.
3.Output can have multiple nodes.
4. Bidirectional edge Traversal means source node to destination node and destination to source can be traversed. Uni directional edge traversal means only source to destination can be traversed but destination to source cannot be traversed. Please check edge traversal during generating relationships in cypher query
5. DO NOT write Node Type in Match instead give it in WHERE clause.
6. In RETURN statement always give appropriate alias for row.
7. For COUNT and aggregator function always return with attribute of NODE Eg. COUNT(q.NODE_ID).
8. MOST IMPORTANT RULE: Strictly use one or multiple different relations in MATCH clause as shown if needed.\n Eg. \"MATCH (q)-[e1]->(a), (a)-[e2]->(p)\" .
9.Generated Cypher query should follow given schema and specified relationships only.
10. If in query user asks for tasks, return name of tasks. If in query user asks for Qgate, return name of Qgate. If query does not contains any of the node type or relationship type for output node, return \"\".
11. If user asks question(s) replace it with Qgate.
12.If exact data is provided in user question generate query. If data provided is ambigous and  node type for given data is not menyioned do not generate the query, only return unspecified_data. unspecified_data should contain explicitly user defined nodes and conditions in list.
13 Always give relationship value in WHERE clause for every relationship defined in MATCH clause. It should also contain list of data for which user has not provided NODE_TYPE or RELATIONSHIP_TYPE explicitly. Any data which is part of pre defined list of Phase and mentioned in user question, treat it as Phase.Predefined List of phases - [\"Prepare\",\"Deployment(Deploy)\",\"Realize\",\"Run\",\"Execute\"]
14. Do not generate the cypher query if no valid relationships exists between the nodes in provided schema.
15.Always analyze the whole schema and use shortest route and minimum relationships to reach output node.

Example of generated Cypher based on user question:
User question: \"What are the Realise phase Qgate questions for topic extensibility?\".
 The generated cypher should follow this query syntax : 
\" MATCH (p)-[e1]->(t), (t)-[e2]->(q) WHERE (p.DESCRIPTION = 'Realize' OR p.NODE_ID = 'Realize') AND p.NODE_TYPE = 'Phase' AND (t.DESCRIPTION = 'extensibility' OR t.NODE_ID = 'extensibility') AND t.NODE_TYPE = 'Topic' AND q.NODE_TYPE = 'Qgate' AND e1.RELATIONSHIP_TYPE = 'HAS_TOPIC_FROM_PHASE' AND e2.RELATIONSHIP_TYPE = 'HAS_QGATE_FROM_TOPIC' RETURN q.DESCRIPTION AS Questions\". 
Example for output_node:
User Question : \"Get all answers for extensibility\".
Here output_node is \"Answer\". Check which node user is expecting as output. \n\nExample for unspecified data :
User Question : Get all answer for clean core. Here, clean core is ambiguous, so it becomes unspecified_data. output_node is answer. Since clean core is ambiguous, generated cypher is empty and eventually io_node_information is also empty. 
Output :{\"cpyher_query\":\"\"io_node_information\":\"None\", \"unspecified_data\":\"[\"clean core\"]\",\"output_node\":\"Answer\"}
Instructions: DO NOT use any other relationship types or properties that are not provided in following schema. 
Schema: {schema}

The question is: {question}. 

Output Format : {output_format}
Do not include any explanations or details in your responses.

 

This is an example prompt. Prompt needs to be changed depending on need and models used.

OUTPUT FORMAT:

 

{
    "cypher_query": "Only Generated cypher query for questions",
    "io_node_information": "Every WHERE clause attribute used in query as key and value as JSON",
    "unspecified_data": "Data which is ambigous and it's NODE_TYPE can be multiple nodes from schema as python list of string engulfed in double quotes.",
    "output_node": "Return user's final ask which matches with NODE_TYPE in schema or else  return "None"."
}

 

 

How to identify unspecified data from query

Let’s understand the problem first.  Consider we get the query “What categories does Monica have?” or “What categories does John Cook have?”. Here we don’t know whether Monica, John Cook is Customer or Supplier. Now both can have categories of products. John Cook being a customer can have products from some categories in his order while Monica being the supplier she can have products form some categories to supply. To solve this problem, we have unspecified data in OUTPUT FORMAT. Here we ask LLM to identify the data which don’t have node type specified. In this case, John Cook or Monica is unspecified data. Now we make similarity search on unspecified data and find the NODE_TYPE of it and replace unspecified data in query with “NODE_TYPE + unspecified data”. So the new query becomes, “What categories does Supplier Monica have?” or “What categories does Customer John Cook have?. Now we again make a call to LLM to generate the open cypher query since the query will be more accurate. Also we have io_node_information in OUTPUT_FORMAT. This we will use to get the correct answer even if there are small typos in query. With the given attributes in WHERE Clause we make similarity search and replace the exact word or description available in database as open cypher uses string search instead of similarity search. output_node in OUTPUT FORMAT can be used to generate a nice descriptive answer. We can again use LLM to generate the answer by providing data retrieved from Database, query and output_node.

CODE:

 

import json, re, os
from hdbcli import dbapi
from gen_ai_hub.proxy.langchain.init_models import init_embedding_model
from gen_ai_hub.proxy import GenAIHubProxyClient
from gen_ai_hub.proxy.langchain.amazon import ChatBedrock
from langchain.schema import AIMessage, HumanMessage, SystemMessage

def replace_where_clause(query, replacement):
    for key, value in replacement.items():
        # Create regex pattern to find the clause to replace
        pattern = rf"({key}\s*=\s*')[^']*(')"
        replacement_string = rf"\1{value}\2"

        # Perform the replacement
        query = re.sub(pattern, replacement_string, query)

    return query

HANA_DB_ADDRESS = "HANA_DB_ADDRESS"
HANA_DB_PORT = "HANA_DB_PORT"
HANA_DB_USER = "HANA_DB_USER"
HANA_DB_PASSWORD ="HANA_DB_PASSWORD"

os.environ["AICORE_CLIENT_ID"] = "AICORE_CLIENT_ID"
os.environ["AICORE_CLIENT_SECRET"] = "AICORE_CLIENT_SECRET"
os.environ["AICORE_AUTH_URL"] = "AICORE_AUTH_URL"
os.environ["AICORE_BASE_URL"] = "AICORE_BASE_URL"
os.environ["AICORE_RESOURCE_GROUP"] = "AICORE_RESOURCE_GROUP"

gen_ai_hub_proxy_client = GenAIHubProxyClient(
    base_url="AICORE_BASE_URL",
    auth_url="AICORE_AUTH_URL",
    client_id="AICORE_CLIENT_ID",
    client_secret="AICORE_CLIENT_SECRET",
    resource_group="AICORE_RESOURCE_GROUP"
)
 
driver = dbapi.connect(
    address=HANA_DB_ADDRESS, 
    port=HANA_DB_PORT, 
    user=HANA_DB_USER, 
    password=HANA_DB_PASSWORD
)

chat_model = ChatBedrock(proxy_client=gen_ai_hub_proxy_client,
                        model_name= "anthropic--claude-3.5-sonnet"
                                     )

cursor = driver.cursor()
schema = """[
                { Node:  
                [  
                { name:NODE_TYPE, data_type: STRING }
                { name:DESCRIPTION, data_type: STRING } ,   
                { name:NODE_ID, data_type: STRING }    
                predefined list of NODE_TYPE:[ 
                                    Customer, 
                                    Order,
                                    Product, 
                                    Category,   
                                    Supplier
                                    ]
                ]   
                }
                ]

                Relationships: 
                [ 
                { CUSTOMER_PLACES_ORDER: [ source: Node(NODE_TYPE: Customer), destination: Node(NODE_TYPE: Order) ], description : ' Customer places one or many orders '}, 
                { ORDER_ASSOCIATED_TO_CUSTOMER: [ source: Node(NODE_TYPE: Order), destination: Node(NODE_TYPE: Customer) ], description : ' Order is associated with Customer '}, 

                { ORDER_HAS_PRODUCTS : [ source: Node(NODE_TYPE: Order), destination: Node(NODE_TYPE: Product) ], description : ' Order contains one or more products '}, 
                { PRODUCT_SUPPLIED_BY: [ source: Node(NODE_TYPE: Product), destination: Node(NODE_TYPE: Supplier) ], description : ' Product is supplied by one or more suppliers '}, 
                { SUPPLIER_SUPPLIES_PRODUCT: [ source: Node(NODE_TYPE: Supplier), destination: Node(NODE_TYPE: Product) ], description : ' Product is supplied by one or more suppliers }, 

                { PRODUCT_BELONGS_TO_CATEGORY: [ source: Node(NODE_TYPE: Product), destination: Node(NODE_TYPE: Category) ], description : ' Product belongs to a specific category '},
                { CATEGORY_HAS_PRODUCTS: [ source: Node(NODE_TYPE: Category), destination: Node(NODE_TYPE: Product) ], description : ' Category has one or more product '},
            ]
"""
output_format = "{\"cypher_query\":\"Only Generated cypher query for questions. If multiple relationship exists, make sure to follow 8th RULE.\",\"io_node_information\":\"Every WHERE clause attribute used in query as key and value as JSON\", \"unspecified_data\":\"Data which is ambigous and it's NODE_TYPE can be multiple nodes from schema as python list of string engulfed in double quotes.\",\"output_node\":\"return user's final ask which matches with NODE_TYPE in schema or else  return \"None\".\"}"
query = "What categories does Monica have?"
question = query
cypher_generation_prompt = f"""Relationships and query may not have exact wordings. RULES: \n1. For the generation of open cypher query use node fields NODE_ID and DESCRIPTION in condition of NODE and generate cypher query only using provided relationships and schema.\n2. There can be chances where, to reach final output one need to traverse multiple nodes.\n3.Output can have multiple nodes.\n4. Bidirectional edge Traversal means source node to destination node and destination to source can be traversed. Uni directional edge traversal means only source to destination can be traversed but destination to source cannot be traversed. Please check edge traversal during generating relationships in cypher query\n5. DO NOT write Node Type in Match instead give it in WHERE clause.\n6. In RETURN statement always give appropriate alias for row.\n7. For COUNT and aggregator function always return with attribute of NODE Eg. COUNT(q.NODE_ID).\n8. MOST IMPORTANT RULE: Strictly use one or multiple different relations in MATCH clause as shown if needed. Do NOT combine two relations even though they are continous.\n Eg. \"MATCH (q)-[e1]->(a), (a)-[e2]->(p)\" .\n9.Generated Cypher query should follow given schema and specified relationships only.\n 10. If in query user asks for tasks, return name of tasks. If in query user asks for Qgate, return name of Qgate. If query does not contains any of the node type or relationship type for output node, return \"\".\n11. If user asks question(s) replace it with Qgate.\n12.If exact data is provided in user question generate query. If data provided is ambigous and  node type for given data is not menyioned do not generate the query, only return unspecified_data. unspecified_data should contain explicitly user defined nodes and conditions in list.\n13 Always give relationship value in WHERE clause for every relationship defined in MATCH clause. It should also contain list of data for which user has not provided NODE_TYPE or RELATIONSHIP_TYPE explicitly. Any data which is part of pre defined list of Phase and mentioned in user question, treat it as Phase.Predefined List of phases - [\"Prepare\",\"Deployment(Deploy)\",\"Realize\",\"Run\",\"Execute\"]\n14. Do not generate the cypher query if no valid relationships exists between the nodes in provided schema.\n15.Always analyze the whole schema and use shortest route and minimum relationships to reach output node.\n\nExample of generated Cypher based on user question:\nUser question: \"What are the Realise phase Qgate questions for topic extensibility?\".\n The generated cypher should follow this query syntax : \" MATCH (p)-[e1]->(t), (t)-[e2]->(q) WHERE (p.DESCRIPTION = 'Realize' OR p.NODE_ID = 'Realize') AND p.NODE_TYPE = 'Phase' AND (t.DESCRIPTION = 'extensibility' OR t.NODE_ID = 'extensibility') AND t.NODE_TYPE = 'Topic' AND q.NODE_TYPE = 'Qgate' AND e1.RELATIONSHIP_TYPE = 'HAS_TOPIC_FROM_PHASE' AND e2.RELATIONSHIP_TYPE = 'HAS_QGATE_FROM_TOPIC' RETURN q.DESCRIPTION AS Questions\". \n\nExample for output_node:\nUser Question : \"Get all answers for extensibility\".\n Here output_node is \"Answer\". Check which node user is expecting as output. \n\nExample for unspecified data :\nUser Question : Which products are associated with Michael Brown?.Here, Michael Brown is ambiguous, so it becomes unspecified_data as we don't know whether Michael Brown is Supplier or Customer. output_node is answer. Since Michael Brown is ambiguous, generated cypher is empty and eventually io_node_information is also empty. Output :\"cpyher_query\":\"\"io_node_information\":\"None\", \"unspecified_data\":\"[\"Michael Brown\"]\",\"ouput_node\":\"Answer\"Instructions:\n 1.DO NOT use any other relationship types or properties that are not provided in following schema.\n2.Do not assume NODE_TYPE of any data in question while generating open cypher query following above RULES. If NODE_TYPE is not mentioned in question, STRICTLY identify that data is unspecified_data.\n3.If no unspecified data then follow RULE no 8, STRICTLY.\nSchema: {schema}\n\nThe question is: {question}. \n\n\nOutput Format : {output_format}\nDo not include any explanations or details in your responses. The output shoud be parsable json object which can be passed to json.loads() in python."""
batch_messages = [
            [
                HumanMessage(content="You are an AI assistant which generates open cypher query using the given schema and its relations only if relation_ship exists between them.While generating query you double check it. Generate Open Cypher statement to query a SAP HANA graph workspace."),
                AIMessage(content= "Provide me the RULES and user question."),
                HumanMessage(content=cypher_generation_prompt)
            ]
    ]
resp = chat_model.generate(batch_messages)
resp = json.loads(resp.model_dump_json())
cypher_query_prompt_response = resp['generations'][0][0]["message"]["content"]
embeddings = init_embedding_model('text-embedding-ada-002')
# cursor = self.db.cursor()

response = json.loads(cypher_query_prompt_response)
attributes = {}
is_where_clause_replacement_needed = True
node_sql_query = f"SELECT TOP 1 *, COSINE_SIMILARITY(NODE_VECTOR, TO_REAL_VECTOR(?)) AS SIMILARITY FROM ERP_NODES ORDER BY COSINE_SIMILARITY(NODE_VECTOR, TO_REAL_VECTOR(?)) DESC;"
if type(response["unspecified_data"]) == str:
    response["unspecified_data"] = json.loads(response["unspecified_data"])

#User query has unspecified data.
if len(response["unspecified_data"]) > 0:
    for entry in response["unspecified_data"]:
        entry_CAPS = entry.upper()
        node_id_query = """SELECT TOP 1 * FROM ERP_NODES WHERE NODE_ID = ?"""
        cursor.execute(node_id_query, (entry_CAPS))
        query_res = cursor.fetchall()
        # Check if unspecified data has matching NODE_ID from our system
        if len(query_res) != 0:
            replacement = str(query_res[0][3]) + " " + str(query_res[0][0])
            # Replace unspecified ID with it's nodetype
            query = query.replace(entry, replacement)
        else:
            # Check if unspecified data has matching Description from our system
            entry_vector = str(embeddings.embed_query(entry))
            cursor.execute(node_sql_query, (entry_vector, entry_vector))
            node_sql_query_response = cursor.fetchall()
            if float(node_sql_query_response[0][5]) > 0.88:
                temp = ""
                temp = node_sql_query_response[0][3] + " " + node_sql_query_response[0][2]
                 # Replace unspecified Description with it's nodetype
                query = query.replace(entry, temp)
    new_question = query
    batch_messages = [
            [
                SystemMessage(content="You are an AI assistant which generates open cypher query using the given schema and its relations only if relation_ship exists between them.While generating query you double check it. Generate Open Cypher statement to query a SAP HANA graph workspace."),
                AIMessage(content= "Provide me the RULES and user question."),
                HumanMessage(content=f"""Relationships and query may not have exact wordings. RULES: \n1. For the generation of open cypher query use node fields NODE_ID and DESCRIPTION in condition of NODE and generate cypher query only using provided relationships and schema.\n2. There can be chances where, to reach final output one need to traverse multiple nodes.\n3.Output can have multiple nodes.\n4. Bidirectional edge Traversal means source node to destination node and destination to source can be traversed. Uni directional edge traversal means only source to destination can be traversed but destination to source cannot be traversed. Please check edge traversal during generating relationships in cypher query\n5. DO NOT write Node Type in Match instead give it in WHERE clause.\n6. In RETURN statement always give appropriate alias for row.\n7. For COUNT and aggregator function always return with attribute of NODE Eg. COUNT(q.NODE_ID).\n8. MOST IMPORTANT RULE: Strictly use one or multiple different relations in MATCH clause as shown if needed. Do NOT combine two relations even though they are continous.\n Eg. \"MATCH (q)-[e1]->(a), (a)-[e2]->(p)\" .\n9.Generated Cypher query should follow given schema and specified relationships only.\n 10. If in query user asks for tasks, return name of tasks. If in query user asks for Qgate, return name of Qgate. If query does not contains any of the node type or relationship type for output node, return \"\".\n11. If user asks question(s) replace it with Qgate.\n12.If exact data is provided in user question generate query. If data provided is ambigous and  node type for given data is not menyioned do not generate the query, only return unspecified_data. unspecified_data should contain explicitly user defined nodes and conditions in list.\n13 Always give relationship value in WHERE clause for every relationship defined in MATCH clause. It should also contain list of data for which user has not provided NODE_TYPE or RELATIONSHIP_TYPE explicitly. Any data which is part of pre defined list of Phase and mentioned in user question, treat it as Phase.Predefined List of phases - [\"Prepare\",\"Deployment(Deploy)\",\"Realize\",\"Run\",\"Execute\"]\n14. Do not generate the cypher query if no valid relationships exists between the nodes in provided schema.\n15.Always analyze the whole schema and use shortest route and minimum relationships to reach output node.\n\nExample of generated Cypher based on user question:\nUser question: \"What are the Realise phase Qgate questions for topic extensibility?\".\n The generated cypher should follow this query syntax : \" MATCH (p)-[e1]->(t), (t)-[e2]->(q) WHERE (p.DESCRIPTION = 'Realize' OR p.NODE_ID = 'Realize') AND p.NODE_TYPE = 'Phase' AND (t.DESCRIPTION = 'extensibility' OR t.NODE_ID = 'extensibility') AND t.NODE_TYPE = 'Topic' AND q.NODE_TYPE = 'Qgate' AND e1.RELATIONSHIP_TYPE = 'HAS_TOPIC_FROM_PHASE' AND e2.RELATIONSHIP_TYPE = 'HAS_QGATE_FROM_TOPIC' RETURN q.DESCRIPTION AS Questions\". \n\nExample for output_node:\nUser Question : \"Get all answers for extensibility\".\n Here output_node is \"Answer\". Check which node user is expecting as output. \n\nExample for unspecified data :\nUser Question : Which products are associated with Michael Brown?.Here, Michael Brown is ambiguous, so it becomes unspecified_data as we don't know whether Michael Brown is Supplier or Customer. output_node is answer. Since Michael Brown is ambiguous, generated cypher is empty and eventually io_node_information is also empty. Output :\"cpyher_query\":\"\"io_node_information\":\"None\", \"unspecified_data\":\"[\"Michael Brown\"]\",\"ouput_node\":\"Answer\"Instructions:\n 1.DO NOT use any other relationship types or properties that are not provided in following schema.\nSchema: {schema}\n\nThe question is: {new_question}. \n\n\nOutput Format : {output_format}\nDo not include any explanations or details in your responses. The output shoud be parsable json object which can be passed to json.loads() in python.""")
            ]
    ]
    # Again make the LLM call to generate the cypher query but with more enhanced data.
    resp = chat_model.generate(batch_messages)
    resp = json.loads(resp.model_dump_json())
    response = resp['generations'][0][0]["message"]["content"]

    response = json.loads(response)
    is_where_clause_replacement_needed = False

else:
    # If there is no unspcified data, make sure that whatever present in WHERE clause of generated cypher query has 
    # correct details as it STRING search to database.
    io_node_information = response["io_node_information"]
    if type(io_node_information) == str:
        io_node_information = io_node_information.replace("'",'"')
        io_node_information = json.loads(io_node_information)
    for key, value in io_node_information.items():
        val = value
        if "DESCRIPTION" in key:
            node_sql_query = f"SELECT TOP 1 *, COSINE_SIMILARITY(NODE_VECTOR, TO_REAL_VECTOR(?)) AS SIMILARITY FROM ERP_NODES ORDER BY COSINE_SIMILARITY(NODE_VECTOR, TO_REAL_VECTOR(?)) DESC;"
            value_vector = str(embeddings.embed_query(value))
            cursor.execute(node_sql_query, (value_vector, value_vector))
            attribute_result = cursor.fetchall()
            if float(attribute_result[0][5]) > 0.88:
                val = attribute_result[0][2]
        attributes.update({key: val})
if is_where_clause_replacement_needed:
    final_cypher_query = replace_where_clause(response["cypher_query"], attributes)
else:
    final_cypher_query = response["cypher_query"]
output_node = response["output_node"]


#Making a Graph Workspace query and retrieving the answer.
if final_cypher_query == '':
    print("Query not generated")
else:
    final_cypher_query = final_cypher_query.replace("'", "''")
    final_sql_query = f"""SELECT * FROM OPENCYPHER_TABLE( GRAPH WORKSPACE "DBADMIN"."ERP" QUERY ' {final_cypher_query} ');"""
    cursor.execute(final_sql_query)
    sql_query_response = cursor.fetchall()
    if len(sql_query_response) == 0:
        print("No data found")
    else:
        db_data = ""
        for row in sql_query_response:
            db_data = db_data + "-"
            db_data = db_data + str(row[0])
            db_data = db_data + "\n"

        final_response = db_data
        print(final_response)
